name: Election
description: Voting systems, historical firsts, and U.S. presidential trivia.

questions.q: What is the academic study of elections, voting patterns, and election results called?

source.notes: From the Greek 'psephos' (pebble) — Athenian voters cast pebbles.

questions.q: What is the ancient method, preferred in Athens, of selecting officials by random lot?

source.notes: Athenians believed elections favored the wealthy and well-connected.

questions.q: What is the term for a single-winner voting system where the candidate with the most votes wins, regardless of majority?

source.notes: Used in U.S. House and Senate elections, UK general elections, etc.

questions.q: What is the practice of voting privately, without revealing one's choice, called?

source.notes: Sometimes called the 'Australian ballot' due to its early adoption there.

questions.q: What kind of election does a U.S. political party hold to choose its general-election candidate?

source.notes: Some states use caucuses instead of primaries.

questions.q: Which 2000 U.S. Supreme Court case ended the Florida recount and effectively decided the presidency?

source.notes: Decided 5-4 in favor of stopping the recount.

questions.q: Who won the popular vote by nearly 3 million but lost the 2016 U.S. presidential election to Donald Trump?

source.notes: Clinton won the popular vote by ~2.87 million.

questions.q: Who is the only U.S. president to serve two non-consecutive terms (until Donald Trump)?

source.notes: 22nd president (1885–1889) and 24th president (1893–1897).

questions.q: Which U.S. president matched Cleveland's non-consecutive feat by winning in 2016, losing in 2020, and winning again in 2024?

source.notes: Trump is the 45th and 47th president.

questions.q: Who won the 1824 popular vote but lost the presidency in the House of Representatives?

source.notes: John Quincy Adams won via the 'Corrupt Bargain'. Jackson came back to win in 1828.

questions.q: Which Republican won the disputed 1876 election after losing the popular vote to Samuel Tilden?

source.notes: Resolved by the Compromise of 1877; ended Reconstruction.

questions.q: What is the minimum age, set by the U.S. Constitution, to serve as U.S. President?

source.original_a: Must be at least 35 years old (others: 14 years' residency, natural-born citizen)
source.notes: Article II, Section 1, Clause 5. Also requires 14 years' residency and natural-born citizenship.

questions.q: Which U.S. president died of pneumonia just 31 days after his inauguration in 1841?

source.notes: Shortest U.S. presidential term ever.

questions.q: Which method has been used to elect popes since the Middle Ages?

source.notes: Cardinals are locked ('cum clavis' = with key) in the Sistine Chapel until they elect a new pope.

questions.q: Which medieval institution selected its emperor through an 'imperial election' by prince-electors?

source.notes: Codified in the Golden Bull of 1356.

questions.q: What 1965 U.S. landmark law dramatically expanded voting access for African Americans in the South?

source.notes: Signed into law by LBJ on August 6, 1965.

questions.q: Which constitutional amendment, ratified in 1920, gave women the right to vote in the United States?

source.notes: Several states had granted women's suffrage before the federal amendment.

questions.q: Which constitutional amendment, ratified in 1971, lowered the U.S. voting age to 18?

source.notes: Vietnam War-era response to 'old enough to fight, old enough to vote.'

questions.q: Which constitutional amendment abolished poll taxes in U.S. federal elections?

source.notes: Ratified 1964.

questions.q: Which Australian state requires voters to cast a ballot in elections (compulsory voting), with a fine for noncompliance?

source.notes: Compulsory voting is a federal law in Australia at federal and state levels — not state-specific. Original answer was incorrect.
